title: Extracting Efficiencies and MTRs
text: metafrontier model fitted, range S3 methods available extract inspect results. vignette demonstrates using simple sfacross + LP example.

title: Extracting Efficiencies and MTRs
code: library(metafrontieR) data("ricephil", package = "sfaR")  ricephil$group <- cut(   ricephil$AREA,   breaks        = quantile(ricephil$AREA, probs = c(0, 1/3, 2/3, 1), na.rm = TRUE),   labels        = c("small", "medium", "large"),   include.lowest = TRUE )  meta_lp <- sfametafrontier(   formula    = log(PROD) ~ log(AREA) + log(LABOR) + log(NPK),   data       = ricephil,   group      = "group",   S          = 1,   udist      = "hnormal",   groupType  = "sfacross",   metaMethod = "lp" )

title: Extracting Efficiencies and MTRs
text: Prints complete model output including group-specific SFA results, metafrontier coefficients (available), efficiency statistics group.
code: summary(meta_lp)

what: efficiencies() — Firm-Level Efficiency and MTR Scores
title: Extracting Efficiencies and MTRs
text: Returns data frame one row per observation containing efficiency estimates metatechnology ratios. estimators available groupType values, though exact columns vary slightly model type.
code: eff <- efficiencies(meta_lp) head(eff)

previous_headings: efficiencies() — Firm-Level Efficiency and MTR Scores
title: Extracting Efficiencies and MTRs
code: # All small farms eff_small <- eff[eff$group == "small", ]  # Descriptive statistics summary(eff_small[, c("TE_group_BC", "TE_meta_BC", "MTR_BC")])  # Group-level means aggregate(cbind(TE_group_BC, TE_meta_BC, MTR_BC) ~ group, data = eff, FUN = mean)

previous_headings: efficiencies() — Firm-Level Efficiency and MTR Scores
title: Extracting Efficiencies and MTRs
code: # MTR distribution by group (base R) boxplot(MTR_BC ~ group, data = eff,         main = "Metatechnology Ratio by Farm Size",         xlab = "Group", ylab = "MTR (BC)",         col  = c("#e8f5ef", "#b2dfdb", "#80cbc4"))  # Histogram of TE_meta_BC hist(eff$TE_meta_BC, breaks = 30,      main = "Distribution of Metafrontier TE",      xlab = "TE_meta_BC", col = "#2d8f65", border = "white")

title: Extracting Efficiencies and MTRs
text: Returns metafrontier coefficient vector (QP SFA methods; NULL LP).
code: # First fit a QP model meta_qp <- sfametafrontier(   formula    = log(PROD) ~ log(AREA) + log(LABOR) + log(NPK),   data       = ricephil, group = "group", S = 1, udist = "hnormal",   groupType  = "sfacross", metaMethod = "qp" ) coef(meta_qp)

title: Extracting Efficiencies and MTRs
text: Returns variance-covariance matrix metafrontier coefficients (models estimate metafrontier parameters).
code: vcov(meta_qp)

title: Extracting Efficiencies and MTRs
text: Returns total log-likelihood value model (sum group-level log-likelihoods plus metafrontier log-likelihood applicable).
code: logLik(meta_lp)

title: Extracting Efficiencies and MTRs
text: Returns three information criteria: AIC, BIC, HQIC.

title: Extracting Efficiencies and MTRs
code: nobs(meta_lp)  # Total observations across all groups

title: Extracting Efficiencies and MTRs
text: Returns fitted frontier values model.
code: fv <- fitted(meta_lp) head(fv)

title: Extracting Efficiencies and MTRs
text: Returns composite error residuals group-level stochastic frontier models.
code: res <- residuals(meta_lp) head(res)

title: Extracting Efficiencies and MTRs
text: can compare information criteria across methods select best model:
code: meta_lp    <- sfametafrontier(log(PROD) ~ log(AREA) + log(LABOR) + log(NPK),                                data = ricephil, group = "group", S = 1,                                udist = "hnormal", groupType = "sfacross",                                metaMethod = "lp") meta_qp    <- sfametafrontier(log(PROD) ~ log(AREA) + log(LABOR) + log(NPK),                                data = ricephil, group = "group", S = 1,                                udist = "hnormal", groupType = "sfacross",                                metaMethod = "qp") meta_huang <- sfametafrontier(log(PROD) ~ log(AREA) + log(LABOR) + log(NPK),                                data = ricephil, group = "group", S = 1,                                udist = "hnormal", groupType = "sfacross",                                metaMethod = "sfa", sfaApproach = "huang")  # Combined information criteria table models <- list(LP = meta_lp, QP = meta_qp, Huang = meta_huang) do.call(rbind, lapply(names(models), function(nm) {   ic_vals <- ic(models[[nm]])   data.frame(Model = nm, AIC = ic_vals[["AIC"]],              BIC = ic_vals[["BIC"]], HQIC = ic_vals[["HQIC"]]) }))

title: Getting Started with metafrontieR
text: metafrontieR implements analyses productivity performance benchmarking across firms operating different technologies. contains routines deterministic envelope estimation via linear quadratic programming, stochastic frontier-based estimation, latent class stochastic frontier estimation, sample selection corrected frontier estimation. Group-level meta-level technical efficiencies, along metatechnology ratios, returned via unified interface. Builds ‘sfaR’ package. classic stochastic frontier analysis (SFA) estimates single frontier firms. Metafrontier analysis extends : Estimating group-specific frontiers technology sub-group. Estimating common metafrontier envelops group frontiers. Computing metatechnology ratios (MTR), quantify far group’s frontier lies best-practice metafrontier. allows researchers disentangle: Technical efficiency relative group’s frontier (TE_group). Metafrontier efficiency relative common best-practice frontier (TE_meta). Metatechnology ratio (MTR = TE_meta / TE_group), captures technologically advanced group’s production possibilities .

title: Getting Started with metafrontieR
text: MTRi=TE_metaiTE_groupi\text{MTR}_i = \frac{\text{TE\_meta}_i}{\text{TE\_group}_i} MTR close 1 means group’s technology near metafrontier (advanced technology). MTR far 1 means group operates less advanced technology.

title: Getting Started with metafrontieR
text: metafrontieR supports four metafrontier estimation methods (metaMethod): three group frontier types (groupType):

title: Getting Started with metafrontieR
text: Note: sfaR automatically installed dependency — need install separately.
code: # Install devtools if needed if (!require("devtools")) install.packages("devtools")  # Install metafrontieR from GitHub devtools::install_github("SulmanOlieko/metafrontieR")

title: Getting Started with metafrontieR
text: minimal example demonstrates core workflow using ricephil dataset sfaR package, three Filipino rice farm-size groups (small, medium, large).

what: Step 1: Load data and create groups
title: Getting Started with metafrontieR
code: library(metafrontieR) data("ricephil", package = "sfaR")  # Create technology groups based on farm area terciles ricephil$group <- cut(   ricephil$AREA,   breaks        = quantile(ricephil$AREA, probs = c(0, 1/3, 2/3, 1), na.rm = TRUE),   labels        = c("small", "medium", "large"),   include.lowest = TRUE ) table(ricephil$group)

title: Getting Started with metafrontieR
code: meta_lp <- sfametafrontier(   formula    = log(PROD) ~ log(AREA) + log(LABOR) + log(NPK),   data       = ricephil,   group      = "group",   S          = 1,            # production frontier (S=1) or cost frontier (S=-1)   udist      = "hnormal",   groupType  = "sfacross",   metaMethod = "lp" )

title: Getting Started with metafrontieR
code: summary(meta_lp)

title: Getting Started with metafrontieR
text: Key output columns:
code: eff <- efficiencies(meta_lp) head(eff)

title: Getting Started with metafrontieR
text: Standard SFA groups → see vignette("sfacross-metafrontier") Unobserved/latent groups → see vignette("sfalcmcross-metafrontier") Sample selection → see vignette("sfaselectioncross-metafrontier") Extracting outputs → see vignette("efficiency-extraction")

title: Standard SFA Metafrontier (groupType =
text: observed group labels available (e.g., farm size, region, ownership type), groupType = "sfacross" fits separate cross-sectional stochastic frontier group using sfaR::sfacross(). group-specific results used estimate common metafrontier using four methods.

title: Standard SFA Metafrontier (groupType =
text: use ricephil dataset sfaR, contains 344 Filipino rice farms. create three technology groups based farm area terciles.
code: library(metafrontieR) data("ricephil", package = "sfaR")  ricephil$group <- cut(   ricephil$AREA,   breaks        = quantile(ricephil$AREA, probs = c(0, 1/3, 2/3, 1), na.rm = TRUE),   labels        = c("small", "medium", "large"),   include.lowest = TRUE )  table(ricephil$group) #>  small medium  large #>    125    104    115

title: Standard SFA Metafrontier (groupType =
text: linear programming (LP) envelope minimises sum absolute deviations group frontier predictions satisfying convexity constraint. stochastic parameters estimated metafrontier . Note: Since LP metafrontier estimated via linear programming, estimated parameters returned metafrontier level. LP envelope fully determined group frontier predictions.
code: meta_lp <- sfametafrontier(   formula    = log(PROD) ~ log(AREA) + log(LABOR) + log(NPK),   data       = ricephil,   group      = "group",   S          = 1,   udist      = "hnormal",   groupType  = "sfacross",   metaMethod = "lp" ) summary(meta_lp)

title: Standard SFA Metafrontier (groupType =
text: quadratic programming (QP) envelope minimises sum squared deviations group frontier predictions. Unlike LP, QP produces smooth envelope differentiable everywhere, returns estimated coefficients standard errors.
code: meta_qp <- sfametafrontier(   formula    = log(PROD) ~ log(AREA) + log(LABOR) + log(NPK),   data       = ricephil,   group      = "group",   S          = 1,   udist      = "hnormal",   groupType  = "sfacross",   metaMethod = "qp" ) summary(meta_qp)

what: Method 3: Stochastic Metafrontier — Huang et al. (2014)
title: Standard SFA Metafrontier (groupType =
text: two-stage stochastic metafrontier Huang, Huang & Liu (2014) uses group-specific fitted frontier values dependent variable second-stage pooled SFA. technology gap U noise V estimated stochastically, naturally bounds metatechnology ratio MTR ∈ (0, 1].
code: meta_huang <- sfametafrontier(   formula     = log(PROD) ~ log(AREA) + log(LABOR) + log(NPK),   data        = ricephil,   group       = "group",   S           = 1,   udist       = "hnormal",   groupType   = "sfacross",   metaMethod  = "sfa",   sfaApproach = "huang" ) summary(meta_huang)

what: Method 4: Stochastic Envelope — O’Donnell et al. (2008)
title: Standard SFA Metafrontier (groupType =
text: O’Donnell et al. (2008) approach uses LP deterministic envelope dependent variable second-stage SFA, rather group-specific fitted values. mixed deterministic–stochastic approach embeds envelope within SFA framework. Warning: MTR values > 1 can arise method second-stage SFA estimates near-zero inefficiency. occurs, consider using metaMethod = "lp" sfaApproach = "huang" instead.
code: meta_ordonnell <- sfametafrontier(   formula     = log(PROD) ~ log(AREA) + log(LABOR) + log(NPK),   data        = ricephil,   group       = "group",   S           = 1,   udist       = "hnormal",   groupType   = "sfacross",   metaMethod  = "sfa",   sfaApproach = "ordonnell" ) summary(meta_ordonnell)

text: four methods use identical group-level estimates. differences arise metafrontier computed:

text: many applications, technology groups firms belong unobserved — directly observe firms operate technology type. latent class model (LCM) addresses : Fitting pooled latent class SFA entire dataset using sfaR implementation based Dakpo et al. (2021), simultaneously estimating class-specific frontier parameters class membership probabilities firm. Assigning firm class based highest posterior class probability. Using assignments technology groups metafrontier. approach appropriate : - observed group variable available. - Technology heterogeneity suspected directly measurable. - priori group boundaries unclear arbitrary.

title: Latent Class SFA Metafrontier (groupType =
text: use utility dataset sfaR, contains 791 observations US electricity utilities. estimate cost frontier explicit group variable.
code: library(metafrontieR) data("utility", package = "sfaR")

title: Sample Selection SFA Metafrontier (groupType =
text: Sample selection bias arises observed sample random draw population. example: firms revenue threshold surveyed. farms adopted technology observed using . Participation programme voluntary, volunteers observed. selection sample correlated firm efficiency, ignoring leads biased frontier estimates. sfaselectioncross implements two-step ML estimator Greene (2010), leveraging sample selection correction provided via sfaR (Dakpo et al. 2022), corrects bias using probit selection equation (Heckman 1979 correction). selection model requires: - binary selection indicator d (1 = selected/observed, 0 = selected). - selection equation formula selectionF specifying variables drive selection. least one variable must appear selectionF main frontier formula. - selected observations (d == 1) participate frontier receive efficiency estimates. Efficiency non-selected observations NA.

title: Sample Selection SFA Metafrontier (groupType =
text: simulate data following approach sfaR documentation:
code: library(metafrontieR)  N <- 2000; set.seed(12345) z1 <- rnorm(N); z2 <- rnorm(N) v1 <- rnorm(N); v2 <- rnorm(N) g  <- rnorm(N) e1 <- v1 e2 <- 0.7071 * (v1 + v2) ds <- z1 + z2 + e1 d  <- ifelse(ds > 0, 1, 0)        # 1 = selected into the sample group <- ifelse(g > 0, 1, 0)      # two technology groups u  <- abs(rnorm(N)) x1 <- abs(rnorm(N)); x2 <- abs(rnorm(N)) y  <- abs(x1 + x2 + e2 - u) dat <- as.data.frame(cbind(y = y, x1 = x1, x2 = x2,                             z1 = z1, z2 = z2, d = d, group = group))  # About 50% of observations are selected table(dat$d) #>    0    1 #> 1013  987

title: Sample Selection SFA Metafrontier (groupType =
text: Note: selectionF argument compulsory groupType = "sfaselectioncross". left-hand side must binary selection variable (d). least one regressor selection equation appear main frontier formula (exclusion restriction identification).
code: meta_sel_lp <- sfametafrontier(   formula    = log(y) ~ log(x1) + log(x2),   selectionF = d ~ z1 + z2,      # selection equation: d is the binary indicator   data       = dat,   group      = "group",   S          = 1L,   udist      = "hnormal",   groupType  = "sfaselectioncross",   modelType  = "greene10",        # Greene (2010) two-step ML correction   lType      = "kronrod",         # integration method for the selection likelihood   Nsub       = 100,               # number of sub-intervals for numerical integration   uBound     = Inf,   method     = "bfgs",   itermax    = 2000,   metaMethod = "lp" ) summary(meta_sel_lp)

what: metafrontieR — Stochastic and Deterministic Metafrontier Analysis
title: metafrontieR — Stochastic and Deterministic Metafrontier Analysis
text: Stochastic Deterministic Metafrontier Analysis Implements analyses productivity performance benchmarking across firms operating different technologies. Contains routines deterministic envelope estimation via linear quadratic programming, stochastic frontier-based estimation, latent class stochastic frontier estimation, sample selection corrected frontier estimation. Group-level meta-level technical efficiencies, along metatechnology ratios, returned via unified interface. Builds ‘sfaR’ package. metafrontieR provides routines : Deterministic envelope metafrontier via linear programming (LP) quadratic programming (QP), following Battese, Rao & O’Donnell (2004) O’Donnell, Rao & Battese (2008). Stochastic two-stage metafrontier following Huang, Huang & Liu (2014). addition, package implements: Latent class stochastic metafrontier analysis — technology groups unobserved, latent class model (LCM) robustly identifies classes routes metafrontier benchmarking following Greene Hensher (2003), Orea Kumbhakar (2004), Greene (2005), Parmeter Kumbhakar (2014). Sample selection correction metafrontier models — corrects sample selection bias following Heckman (1979), Greene (2010), Greene (2003). Dependency: metafrontieR depends sfaR package Dakpo, Desjeux & Latruffe (2023), provides underlying stochastic frontier estimation routines group-level models.

title: metafrontieR — Stochastic and Deterministic Metafrontier Analysis
text: Note need install sfaR manually, metafrontieR takes care automatically.
code: # Install devtools if not already installed if (!require("devtools")) install.packages("devtools")  # Install metafrontieR from GitHub devtools::install_github("SulmanOlieko/metafrontieR")

title: metafrontieR — Stochastic and Deterministic Metafrontier Analysis
text: following sections provide comprehensive examples covering three group-level model types four metafrontier methods. section demonstrates full workflow: data preparation → group frontier estimation → metafrontier → efficiency MTR extraction.

title: metafrontieR — Stochastic and Deterministic Metafrontier Analysis
text: Let’s use ricephil data sfaR. data, group boundaries observed (farm-size variable). assume production technology varies farm size, can try estimate three frontiers correspond three types farm sizes, namely small, medium large. can create group variable group captures groups. can estimate group’s frontier separately using sfacross sfaR package. , specify option groupType  = "sfacross" sfametafrontier().

title: metafrontieR — Stochastic and Deterministic Metafrontier Analysis

code: library(metafrontieR) data("ricephil", package = "sfaR")  # Create three technology groups based on farm area terciles ricephil$group <- cut(ricephil$AREA,   breaks = quantile(ricephil$AREA, probs = c(0, 1/3, 2/3, 1), na.rm = TRUE),   labels = c("small", "medium", "large"),   include.lowest = TRUE ) table(ricephil$group) small medium  large    125    104    115

title: metafrontieR — Stochastic and Deterministic Metafrontier Analysis
text: can begin estimating deterministic linear programming envelope (Battese, Rao & O’Donnell, 2004) three group frontiers. metafrontier parameter vector minimises sum absolute deviations staying group frontier predictions. using Cobb-Douglas functional form rice production PROD response variable, AREA, LABOR NPK inputs. Note: Since metafrontier estimated via linear programming, estimated parameters returned. harvest individual efficiency, metafrontier efficiency MTR estimates:

title: metafrontieR — Stochastic and Deterministic Metafrontier Analysis
text: can also estimate quadratic programming envelope minimises sum squared deviations group frontier predictions subject envelope constraint. now switch metaMethod = "qp". expected, two approaches produce almost identical outputs. Note: estimation group frontiers use method, sfacross, difference compute metafrontier. QP estimates deterministic frontier, stochastic variance parameters returned.

what: 1c. Two-stage SFA Metafrontier — Huang et al. (2014) (sfaApproach = "huang")
title: metafrontieR — Stochastic and Deterministic Metafrontier Analysis
text: approach, group-specific fitted frontier values pooled together serve dependent variable second-stage pooled SFA. technology gap U noise V estimated stochastically.

what: 1d. Two-stage SFA Metafrontier — O’Donnell et al. (2008) (sfaApproach = "ordonnell")
title: metafrontieR — Stochastic and Deterministic Metafrontier Analysis
text: approach, LP deterministic envelope used response variable second stage SFA quantifies stochastic variation around envelope. O’Donnell et al. (2008) approach involves taking deterministic envelope (maximum) group frontier values data point (yMeta = apply(groupFrontierMat, 1, max)). new yMeta surface maximum several hyperplanes, shape purely convex completely lacks statistical noise. second-stage sfacross tries fit single straight line (hyperplane) convex envelope, residuals predominantly curve upward away line. production frontiers, results right-skewed OLS residuals. SFA models naturally interpret right-skewed residuals near-zero inefficiency (sigma_u -> 0). meta-inefficiency estimated near zero (u_meta -> 0), meta-efficiency approaches 1 (TE_meta ~= 1). TE_meta = TE_group * MTR, get MTR = TE_meta / TE_group. Since TE_meta ~1 TE_group < 1, mathematically forces MTR > 1. happen? fitting stochastic frontier via Maximum Likelihood onto purely mathematical LP envelope strictly enforce bounding constraint MTR <= 1 every individual data point. SFA line inevitably cut LP envelope rather sitting strictly points. well-known theoretical computational limitation producing MTR value(s) > 1, partly Huang et al. (2014) proposed alternative method. solve ? exactly warning message added code. require MTR <= 1 bounds: (1) Use metaMethod = “lp” “qp” (explicitly enforce mathematical envelope constraints). (2) Use sfaApproach = “huang”, avoids “wrong skewness” problem using actual observations’ -group fitted values (yhat_group) SFA dependent variable, directly estimating technology gap U_i, inherently bounding MTR = exp(-U_i) <= 1.

what: Section 3: Sample Selection SFA Group Frontier (groupType = "sfaselectioncross")
title: metafrontieR — Stochastic and Deterministic Metafrontier Analysis
text: observed sample random (e.g., firms revenue threshold surveyed), sample selection bias can distort frontier estimates. sfaselectioncross corrects using two-step approach Greene (2010). selected observations (d == 1) participate frontier metafrontier; efficiency estimates non-selected observations NA. simulated example (adapted sfaR):

previous_headings: Section 3: Sample Selection SFA Group Frontier (groupType = "sfaselectioncross")
title: metafrontieR — Stochastic and Deterministic Metafrontier Analysis
code: N <- 2000; set.seed(12345) z1 <- rnorm(N); z2 <- rnorm(N) v1 <- rnorm(N); v2 <- rnorm(N) g  <- rnorm(N) e1 <- v1 e2 <- 0.7071 * (v1 + v2) ds <- z1 + z2 + e1 d  <- ifelse(ds > 0, 1, 0)        # binary selection indicator: 1 = selected group <- ifelse(g > 0, 1, 0)      # two technology groups u  <- abs(rnorm(N)) x1 <- abs(rnorm(N)); x2 <- abs(rnorm(N)) y  <- abs(x1 + x2 + e2 - u) dat <- as.data.frame(cbind(y=y, x1=x1, x2=x2, z1=z1, z2=z2, d=d, group=group)) # About 50% of observations are selected: table(dat$d) 0    1   1013  987

title: Compute efficiency estimates and metatechnology ratios from stochastic metafrontier models — efficiencies
text: efficiencies returns efficiency estimates metatechnology ratio (MTR) measures objects class "sfametafrontier" returned sfametafrontier. function supports models estimated via linear programming (LP), quadratic programming (QP), stochastic second-stage SFA ("sfa"), observation computes group-specific technical efficiency, metafrontier technical efficiency, metatechnology ratio (MTR), using Jondrow, Lovell, Materov, Schmidt (1982) (JLMS) Battese Coelli (1988) (BC) estimators. Additional model-specific columns returned depending groupType.

title: Compute efficiency estimates and metatechnology ratios from stochastic metafrontier models — efficiencies
text: object object class "sfametafrontier" returned sfametafrontier. level number strictly 0 0.9999 specifying nominal coverage (-)efficiency confidence intervals. Default 0.95. argument passed underlying efficiencies method group-level model (class "sfacross", "sfalcmcross", "sfaselectioncross"). newData Optional data frame --sample prediction efficiency estimates. NULL (default), efficiencies computed observations used estimation. ... arguments (currently ignored).

title: Compute efficiency estimates and metatechnology ratios from stochastic metafrontier models — efficiencies
text: data frame one row per observation (original row order), containing following columns. exact set columns depends groupType: Columns present model types: id Observation identifier. Contains row name observation appeared data supplied sfametafrontier. data frame explicit row names, sequential integers ("1", "2", ...) used. column always first column returned data frame. <group> Group_c technology group identifier observation. groupType = "sfacross" "sfaselectioncross", column takes name user-supplied group variable contains group label observation belongs. groupType = "sfalcmcross", named Group_c contains integer index latent class assigned maximum posterior probability criterion. u_g Group-specific conditional mean inefficiency term, computed \(E[u_i \mid \varepsilon_i]\). JLMS (Jondrow, Lovell, Materov, Schmidt, 1982) point estimate inefficiency group-frontier level. groupType = "sfaselectioncross", u_g NA observations selected sample (selection indicator = 0). TE_group_JLMS Group-specific technical efficiency using Jondrow, Lovell, Materov, Schmidt (1982) estimator: \(TE^g_i = \exp(-E[u_i \mid \varepsilon_i])\). groupType = "sfaselectioncross", NA non-selected observations. TE_group_BC Group-specific technical efficiency using Battese Coelli (1988) estimator: \(TE^g_i = E[\exp(-u_i) \mid \varepsilon_i]\). groupType = "sfaselectioncross", NA non-selected observations. TE_group_BC_reciprocal Reciprocal Battese Coelli (1988) group technical efficiency: \(1 / TE^{g,BC}_i\). production frontiers equals cost-efficiency index implied BC estimator. Present three model types. groupType = "sfaselectioncross", NA non-selected observations. u_meta Metafrontier inefficiency, measuring technology-gap component \(U_i \ge 0\) separates group frontier global metafrontier. Computed second-stage SFA metaMethod = "sfa", derived LP/QP gap \(U_i = \max\{S \cdot (\ln \hat{y}^*_i - \ln \hat{y}^g_i), 0\}\) metaMethod = "lp" "qp". TE_meta_JLMS Metafrontier technical efficiency based JLMS group efficiency: \(TE^*_{JLMS,} = TE^g_{JLMS,} \times MTR_{JLMS,}\). TE_meta_BC Metafrontier technical efficiency based Battese Coelli (1988) group efficiency: \(TE^*_{BC,} = TE^g_{BC,} \times MTR_{BC,}\). MTR_JLMS Metatechnology ratio computed using JLMS group efficiency: \(MTR_{JLMS,} = TE^*_{JLMS,} / TE^g_{JLMS,} = \exp(-U_i)\). Values range 0 1. value 1 indicates group frontier observation coincides metafrontier. MTR_BC Metatechnology ratio computed using Battese Coelli (1988) group efficiency: \(MTR_{BC,} = TE^*_{BC,} / TE^g_{BC,} = \exp(-U_i)\). Additional columns groupType = "sfacross" : uLB_g, uUB_g Lower upper bounds level confidence interval conditional mean inefficiency u_g, constructed using asymptotic distribution conditional estimator. Available distributions closed-form expressions confidence bounds, udist = "hnormal" udist = "tnormal". m_g Mode conditional distribution one-sided error term \(u_i \mid \varepsilon_i\). alternative point estimate inefficiency. Available distributions conditional mode closed-form expression. TE_group_mode Group-specific technical efficiency evaluated conditional mode: \(TE^g_{\mathrm{mode},} = \exp(-m_i)\). teBCLB_g, teBCUB_g Lower upper bounds level confidence interval Battese Coelli (1988) group technical efficiency TE_group_BC. Constructed corresponding bounds conditional distribution \(\exp(-u_i \mid \varepsilon_i)\). Additional columns groupType = "sfalcmcross" : PosteriorProb_c Posterior probability observation \(\) belongs assigned class (one highest posterior probability). Computed via Bayes' rule \(P(j \mid y_i, x_i) \propto \pi(,j) \, P(\mid j)\), \(\pi(,j)\) prior class probability \(P(\mid j)\) class-conditional likelihood. PosteriorProb_cJ (per class, \(J = 1, 2, \ldots\)) Posterior probability belonging latent class \(J\), computed via Bayes' rule class separately. One column produced estimated class. PriorProb_cJ (per class, \(J = 1, 2, \ldots\)) Prior (unconditional) probability belonging latent class \(J\), given logistic specification \(\pi(,J) = \exp(\bm{\theta}_J'\mathbf{Z}_{hi}) /       \sum_m \exp(\bm{\theta}_m'\mathbf{Z}_{hi})\). u_cJ (per class, \(J = 1, 2, \ldots\)) Conditional mean inefficiency term class \(J\): \(E[u_{\mid J} \mid \varepsilon_{\mid J}]\). teBC_cJ (per class, \(J = 1, 2, \ldots\)) Battese Coelli (1988) technical efficiency class \(J\): \(E[\exp(-u_{\mid J}) \mid \varepsilon_{\mid J}]\). teBC_reciprocal_cJ (per class, \(J = 1, 2, \ldots\)) Reciprocal class-\(J\) Battese Coelli (1988) efficiency: \(1/TE^{BC}_{\mid J}\). ineff_cJ (per class, \(J = 1, 2, \ldots\)) Inefficiency estimate observation restricted class \(J\) (.e. value assigned class observation belong; NA classes). effBC_cJ (per class, \(J = 1, 2, \ldots\)) Battese Coelli (1988) efficiency observation's assigned class; NA non-assigned classes. ReffBC_cJ (per class, \(J = 1, 2, \ldots\)) Reciprocal Battese Coelli (1988) efficiency observation's assigned class; NA non-assigned classes.

title: Compute efficiency estimates and metatechnology ratios from stochastic metafrontier models — efficiencies
text: group, group-level frontier model estimated maximising log-likelihood using distribution specified udist sfametafrontier. Given estimated composite error \(\varepsilon_i = v_i - Su_i\), conditional distribution \(u_i \mid \varepsilon_i\) used compute: JLMS estimator (Jondrow, Lovell, Materov, Schmidt, 1982): \(\hat{u}_i = E[u_i \mid \varepsilon_i]\), \(TE^g_{JLMS,} = \exp(-\hat{u}_i)\); BC estimator (Battese Coelli, 1988): \(TE^g_{BC,} = E[\exp(-u_i) \mid \varepsilon_i]\); mode estimator: \(m_i = \mathrm{mode}[u_i \mid \varepsilon_i]\), \(TE^g_{\mathrm{mode},} = \exp(-m_i)\); confidence bounds \(u_i\) \(TE^g_{BC,}\) nominal level level. groupType = "sfaselectioncross", estimates NA observations selected sample (binary selection indicator equal 0). groupType = "sfalcmcross", composite efficiencies u_g, TE_group_JLMS, TE_group_BC computed using posterior-probability-weighted class assignments.

title: Compute efficiency estimates and metatechnology ratios from stochastic metafrontier models — efficiencies
text: MTR measures far group frontier lies metafrontier observation. Let \(\ln \hat{y}^g_i\) group-specific fitted frontier value \(\ln \hat{y}^*_i\) metafrontier fitted value. metaMethod = "lp" "qp" (Battese, Rao, O'Donnell, 2004): $$MTR_i = \exp\!\bigl(           -\max\!\bigl\{S \cdot (\ln \hat{y}^*_i - \ln \hat{y}^g_i),\, 0\bigr\}         \bigr)$$ \(S = 1\) production/profit frontiers \(S = -1\) cost frontiers. technology gap \(U_i = \max\{S \cdot (\ln \hat{y}^*_i - \ln \hat{y}^g_i), 0\}\) stored u_meta. metaMethod = "sfa" sfaApproach = "huang" (Huang, Huang, Liu, 2014): $$MTR_i = TE^*_i = \exp(-U_i)$$ \(U_i\) one-sided error term second-stage SFA. metaMethod = "sfa" sfaApproach = "ordonnell" (O'Donnell, Rao, Battese, 2008): \(MTR_i = TE^{*,\mathrm{sfa}}_i / TE^g_i\), \(TE^{*,\mathrm{sfa}}_i\) technical efficiency second-stage SFA fitted LP envelope values. metafrontier technical efficiency : $$TE^*_i = TE^g_i \times MTR_i$$ computed separately JLMS BC group efficiency estimators. MTR_JLMS MTR_BC reported, distinguishing group-level efficiency estimator used basis.

title: Stochastic metafrontier estimation — sfametafrontier
text: sfametafrontier estimates stochastic metafrontier model cross-sectional pooled data. function follows theoretical frameworks Battese, Rao, O'Donnell (2004) O'Donnell, Rao, Battese (2008), additionally implements two-stage stochastic approach Huang, Huang, Liu (2014). Three types group-level frontier models supported: standard stochastic frontier analysis (sfacross), sample selection stochastic frontier analysis (sfaselectioncross), latent class stochastic frontier analysis (sfalcmcross).

title: Stochastic metafrontier estimation — sfametafrontier
text: formula symbolic description frontier model estimated, based generic function formula. groupType = "sfaselectioncross", argument specifies frontier (outcome) equation must standard formula whose left-hand side output (cost) variable whose right-hand side contains frontier regressors (see also selectionF). muhet one-part formula account heterogeneity mean pre-truncated normal distribution. Applicable groupType = "sfacross" udist = "tnormal". variables specified model conditional mean \(\mu_i = \bm{\omega}'\mathbf{Z}_{\mu}\) truncated normal inefficiency distribution (see section ‘Details’). uhet one-part formula account heteroscedasticity one-sided error variance. Applicable three model types. variance inefficiency term modelled \(\sigma^2_u = \exp(\bm{\delta}'\mathbf{Z}_u)\), \(\mathbf{Z}_u\) inefficiency drivers \(\bm{\delta}\) associated coefficients (see section ‘Details’). vhet one-part formula account heteroscedasticity two-sided error variance. Applicable three model types. variance noise term modelled \(\sigma^2_v = \exp(\bm{\phi}'\mathbf{Z}_v)\), \(\mathbf{Z}_v\) heteroscedasticity variables \(\bm{\phi}\) coefficients (see section ‘Details’). thet one-part formula account technological heterogeneity construction latent classes. Applicable groupType = "sfalcmcross". variables specified enter logit formulation determines prior class membership probabilities \(\pi(,j)\) (see section ‘Details’). logDepVar Logical. Informs whether dependent variable logged (TRUE) (FALSE). Default TRUE. Must match transformation applied left-hand side formula. data data frame containing variables referenced formula, selectionF, muhet, uhet, vhet, thet, group. subset optional vector specifying subset observations used estimation process. weights optional vector weights used weighted log-likelihood estimation. NULL numeric vector strictly positive values. NULL (default), observations receive equal weight. wscale Logical. weights NULL, scaling transformation applied weights sum sample size: $$w_{\mathrm{new}} = n \times     \frac{w_{\mathrm{old}}}{\sum w_{\mathrm{old}}}$$ Default TRUE. FALSE, raw weights used without scaling. group Character string. name column data identifying technology group observation. column coerced factor internally must least two unique values. groupType = "sfalcmcross" group NULL, single pooled latent class model estimated class assignments serve groups (see section ‘Details’). S Integer. Frontier orientation. S = 1 (default): production profit frontier, \(\varepsilon_i = v_i - u_i\). S = -1: cost frontier, \(\varepsilon_i = v_i + u_i\). udist Character string. Distribution one-sided error term \(u_i \ge 0\). following distributions available groupType = "sfacross": "hnormal" (default): half-normal distribution (Aigner et al., 1977; Meeusen van den Broeck, 1977). "exponential": exponential distribution. "tnormal": truncated normal distribution (Stevenson, 1980). "rayleigh": Rayleigh distribution (Hajargasht, 2015). "uniform": uniform distribution (Li, 1996; Nguyen, 2010). "gamma": Gamma distribution, estimated maximum simulated likelihood (Greene, 2003). "lognormal": log-normal distribution, estimated maximum simulated likelihood (Migon Medici, 2001; Wang Ye, 2020). "weibull": Weibull distribution, estimated maximum simulated likelihood (Tsionas, 2007). "genexponential": generalised exponential distribution (Papadopoulos, 2020). "tslaplace": truncated skewed Laplace distribution (Wang, 2012). groupType = "sfaselectioncross" "sfalcmcross", "hnormal" currently supported. start Numeric vector. Optional starting values maximum likelihood (ML) maximum simulated likelihood (MSL) estimation group-level frontier models. NULL (default), starting values computed automatically. groupType = "sfacross", derived OLS residuals. groupType = "sfalcmcross", depend whichStart. scaling Logical. Applicable groupType = "sfacross" udist = "tnormal". TRUE, scaling property model (Wang Schmidt, 2002) estimated, whereby \(u_i = h(\mathbf{Z}_u, \bm{\delta}) u^*_i\) \(u^*_i\) follows truncated normal distribution \(N^+(\tau, \exp(c_u))\). Default FALSE. modelType Character string. Applicable groupType = "sfaselectioncross". Specifies model used correct selection bias. Currently, "greene10" (default) supported, corresponding two-step approach Greene (2010): probit model estimated selection equation, inverse Mills ratio included correction term stochastic frontier second step. groupType Character string. Type frontier model estimated technology group. Three options available: "sfacross" (default): standard cross-sectional stochastic frontier analysis (sfacross). Groups defined group variable. 10 distributions udist supported, along heteroscedasticity error components (uhet, vhet), heterogeneity truncated mean (muhet), scaling property. "sfaselectioncross": sample selection stochastic frontier analysis (sfaselectioncross). Corrects sample selection bias via generalised Heckman approach (Greene, 2010). Requires selectionF. observations selection indicator equals one enter frontier metafrontier; efficiency estimates non-selected observations NA. udist = "hnormal" supported. "sfalcmcross": latent class stochastic frontier analysis (sfalcmcross). Estimates finite mixture frontier models number classes determined lcmClasses. group supplied, separate latent class model estimated per group-stratum combined metafrontier. group omitted, single pooled model estimated class assignments serve technology groups. Supports thet class-membership covariates uhet, vhet within-class heteroscedasticity. udist = "hnormal" supported. metaMethod Character string. Method estimating global metafrontier envelopes group frontiers. Three options available: "lp" (default): deterministic linear programming envelope. Finds parameter vector \(\bm{\beta}^*\) minimising \(\sum_i |\ln \hat{f}(x_i, \bm{\beta}^*) - \ln \hat{f}(x_i,       \hat{\bm{\beta}}_{(g)})|\) subject \(\ln \hat{f}(x_i, \bm{\beta}^*) \ge \ln \hat{f}(x_i,       \hat{\bm{\beta}}_{(g)})\) observations groups (Battese et al., 2004). "qp": deterministic quadratic programming envelope. Minimises sum squared deviations envelope constraint. "sfa": stochastic metafrontier estimated second-stage pooled SFA. specific construction dependent variable determined sfaApproach. sfaApproach Character string. Applicable metaMethod = "sfa". Determines second-stage SFA constructed: "ordonnell" (default): LP envelope group frontier predicted values re-estimated stochastic frontier, following O'Donnell, Rao, Battese (2008). second-stage SFA directly targets global technology envelope. "huang": group-specific fitted frontier value \(\ln \hat{y}^g_i\) observation used dependent variable pooled cross-sectional SFA (Huang, Huang, Liu, 2014). technology gap \(U_i \ge 0\) second-stage noise \(V_i\) estimated directly SFA procedure. "ordonnell": column-wise maximum group-fitted frontier values (deterministic LP envelope) used dependent variable second-stage SFA (O'Donnell, Rao, Battese, 2008). selectionF two-sided formula specifying sample selection equation, e.g., selected ~ z1 + z2. left-hand side must binary (0/1) indicator already present data: 1 means observation participates frontier metafrontier; 0 means excluded (efficiency estimates NA). Alternatively, named list formulas, one per group level, may supplied allow group-specific selection equations. Required groupType = "sfaselectioncross"; ignored otherwise. lcmClasses Integer. Number latent classes estimated per group groupType = "sfalcmcross". Must 2 5 (default 2). optimal number classes can selected based information criteria (see ic). whichStart Integer. Strategy obtaining starting values latent class model (groupType = "sfalcmcross"): 1: starting values obtained method moments. 2 (default): model initialised first solving homoscedastic pooled cross-sectional SFA using algorithm specified initAlg initIter iterations. initAlg Character string. Optimisation algorithm used initialisation latent class model whichStart = 2. algorithms maxLik package supported: "nm" (default): Nelder-Mead (see maxNM). "bfgs": Broyden-Fletcher-Goldfarb-Shanno (see maxBFGS). "bhhh": Berndt-Hall-Hall-Hausman (see maxBHHH). "nr": Newton-Raphson (see maxNR). "cg": Conjugate Gradient (see maxCG). "sann": Simulated Annealing (see maxSANN). initIter Integer. Maximum number iterations initialisation algorithm whichStart = 2 groupType = "sfalcmcross". Default 100. lType Character string. Specifies likelihood evaluated selection model (groupType = "sfaselectioncross"). Five options available: "ghermite" (default): Gauss-Hermite quadrature (see gaussHermiteData). "kronrod": Gauss-Kronrod quadrature (see integrate). "hcubature": adaptive integration hypercubes (see hcubature). "pcubature": p-adaptive cubature (see pcubature). "msl": maximum simulated likelihood (controlled simType, Nsim, prime, burn, antithetics, seed). Nsub Integer. Number quadrature nodes integration subdivisions lType "ghermite", "kronrod", "hcubature", "pcubature". Applicable groupType = "sfaselectioncross". Default 100. uBound Numeric. Upper bound numerical integration inefficiency component lType "kronrod", "hcubature", "pcubature". Gauss-Hermite bound automatically infinite. Applicable groupType = "sfaselectioncross". Default Inf. intol Numeric. Integration tolerance quadrature approaches "kronrod", "hcubature", "pcubature". Applicable groupType = "sfaselectioncross". Default 1e-6. method Character string. Optimisation algorithm main ML/MSL estimation group-level frontier model. Default "bfgs". Eleven algorithms available: "bfgs": Broyden-Fletcher-Goldfarb-Shanno (see maxBFGS). "bhhh": Berndt-Hall-Hall-Hausman (see maxBHHH). "nr": Newton-Raphson (see maxNR). "nm": Nelder-Mead (see maxNM). "cg": Conjugate Gradient (see maxCG). "sann": Simulated Annealing (see maxSANN). "ucminf": quasi-Newton optimisation BFGS updating inverse Hessian soft line search (see ucminf). "mla": Marquardt-Levenberg algorithm (see mla). "sr1": Symmetric Rank 1 trust-region method (see trust.optim). "sparse": trust-region method sparse Hessian (see trust.optim). "nlminb": PORT routines optimisation (see nlminb). hessianType Integer. Specifies Hessian returned group-level frontier estimation. accepted values match underlying sfaR function groupType: groupType = "sfacross": 1 (default), analytic Hessian returned; 2, BHHH Hessian \(\mathbf{G}'\mathbf{G}\) estimated. groupType = "sfalcmcross": 1 (default), analytic Hessian returned; 2, BHHH Hessian estimated. groupType = "sfaselectioncross": 1, analytic Hessian returned; 2 (default), BHHH Hessian \(\mathbf{G}'\mathbf{G}\) estimated. BHHH default reflects two-step nature selection estimator. NULL (package default), group-level model uses natural default corresponding sfaR function, ensuring standard errors computed sfametafrontier identical standalone sfaR call group subset. simType Character string. Simulation method maximum simulated likelihood (MSL). Applicable groupType = "sfacross" udist "gamma", "lognormal", "weibull", groupType = "sfaselectioncross" lType = "msl": "halton" (default): Halton quasi-random sequences. "ghalton": Generalised-Halton sequences. "sobol": Sobol low-discrepancy sequences. "uniform": pseudo-random uniform draws. Nsim Integer. Number simulation draws MSL. Default 100. prime Integer. Prime number used construct Halton Generalised-Halton sequences. Default 2. burn Integer. Number leading draws discarded Halton sequence reduce serial correlation. Default 10. antithetics Logical. TRUE, antithetic draws added: first Nsim/2 draws taken, remaining Nsim/2 \(1 - \text{draw}\). Default FALSE. seed Integer. Random seed simulation draws, ensuring reproducibility MSL estimates. Default 12345. itermax Integer. Maximum number iterations main optimisation. Default 2000. method = "sann", recommended increase substantially (e.g., itermax = 20000). printInfo Logical. TRUE, optimisation progress printed estimation group-level model. Default FALSE. tol Numeric. Convergence tolerance. algorithm considered converged change log-likelihood successive iterations smaller tol absolute value. Default 1e-12. gradtol Numeric. Gradient convergence tolerance. algorithm considered converged Euclidean norm gradient smaller gradtol. Default 1e-6. stepmax Numeric. Maximum step length used "ucminf" algorithm. Default 0.1. qac Character string. Quadratic Approximation Correction "bhhh" "nr" algorithms Hessian negative definite: "marquardt" (default): step length decreased also shifting closer gradient direction. "stephalving": step length halved, preserving current direction. See maxBHHH maxNR details. ... Additional arguments passed second-stage SFA call metaMethod = "sfa". x object class "sfametafrontier", returned sfametafrontier, use print method.

title: Stochastic metafrontier estimation — sfametafrontier
text: stochastic frontier model defined : $$y_i = \alpha + \mathbf{x}_i'\bm{\beta} + v_i - Su_i$$ \(y\) output (cost, revenue, profit), \(\mathbf{x}\) vector frontier regressors, \(u_i \ge 0\) one-sided inefficiency term variance \(\sigma^2_u\), \(v_i\) symmetric noise term variance \(\sigma^2_v\). Estimation ML distributions except "gamma", "lognormal", "weibull", MSL used Halton, Generalised-Halton, Sobol, uniform draws. Antithetic draws available uniform case. account heteroscedasticity, variances modelled \(\sigma^2_u = \exp(\bm{\delta}'\mathbf{Z}_u)\) \(\sigma^2_v = \exp(\bm{\phi}'\mathbf{Z}_v)\). truncated normal distribution, heterogeneity pre-truncation mean modelled \(\mu_i = \bm{\omega}'\mathbf{Z}_{\mu}\). scaling property (Wang Schmidt, 2002) can also imposed truncated normal.

title: Stochastic metafrontier estimation — sfametafrontier
text: model extends Heckman (1979) selection framework stochastic frontier setting (Greene, 2010; Dakpo et al., 2021). selection frontier equations : $$y_{1i}^* = \mathbf{Z}_{si}'\bm{\gamma} + w_i, \quad     w_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$$ $$y_{2i}^* = \mathbf{x}_i'\bm{\beta} + v_i - Su_i$$ \(y_{1i} = \mathbf{1}(y_{1i}^* > 0)\) binary selection indicator \(y_{2i} = y_{2i}^*\) observed \(y_{1i} = 1\). Selection bias arises \(\rho = \mathrm{Corr}(w_i, v_i) \ne 0\). selected observations enter frontier metafrontier estimation; efficiency estimates non-selected observations NA.

title: Stochastic metafrontier estimation — sfametafrontier
text: latent class model (Orea Kumbhakar, 2004) estimates finite mixture \(J\) frontier models: $$y_i = \alpha_j + \mathbf{x}_i'\bm{\beta}_j + v_{|j} - Su_{|j}$$ prior class probability follows logit specification: $$\pi(,j) = \frac{\exp(\bm{\theta}_j'\mathbf{Z}_{hi})}     {\sum_{m=1}^{J}\exp(\bm{\theta}_m'\mathbf{Z}_{hi})}$$ Class assignment based maximum posterior probability computed via Bayes' rule. group omitted, single pooled model estimated class assignments serve technology groups.

title: Stochastic metafrontier estimation — sfametafrontier
text: global metafrontier \(f(x_i, \bm{\beta}^*)\) envelopes group frontiers. LP (Battese et al., 2004), \(\bm{\beta}^*\) minimises \(\sum_i |\ln \hat{f}(x_i, \bm{\beta}^*) - \ln \hat{f}(x_i,   \hat{\bm{\beta}}_{(g)})|\) subject \(\ln \hat{f}(x_i, \bm{\beta}^*) \ge \ln \hat{f}(x_i,   \hat{\bm{\beta}}_{(g)})\). QP minimises squared analogue. stochastic approaches (Huang et al., 2014; O'Donnell et al., 2008) treat technology gap \(U_i\) one-sided error second-stage SFA. Group metafrontier efficiencies : $$TE_i^g = \exp(-u_i^g), \quad     MTR_i = \exp(-U_i), \quad     TE_i^* = TE_i^g \times MTR_i$$ Jondrow et al. (1982) Battese Coelli (1988) estimators provided measure. See efficiencies details.

title: Stochastic metafrontier estimation — sfametafrontier
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ ########################################################################### ## -------- SECTION 1: Standard SFA Group Frontier ----------------------## ## Using the rice production dataset (ricephil) from Battese et al.      ## ## Groups are formed based on farm area terciles (small/medium/large).   ## ###########################################################################  data("ricephil", package = "sfaR") ricephil$group <- cut(ricephil$AREA,   breaks = quantile(ricephil$AREA, probs = c(0, 1 / 3, 2 / 3, 1), na.rm = TRUE),   labels = c("small", "medium", "large"),   include.lowest = TRUE )  ## 1a. sfacross groups + LP metafrontier ##     Deterministic envelope via linear programming (Battese et al., 2004). meta_sfacross_lp <- sfametafrontier(   formula    = log(PROD) ~ log(AREA) + log(LABOR) + log(NPK),   data       = ricephil,   group      = "group",   S          = 1,   udist      = "hnormal",   groupType  = "sfacross",   metaMethod = "lp" ) summary(meta_sfacross_lp) # Retrieve individual efficiency and metatechnology ratio estimates: ef_lp <- efficiencies(meta_sfacross_lp) head(ef_lp)  ## 1b. sfacross groups + QP metafrontier ##     Deterministic envelope via quadratic programming. meta_sfacross_qp <- sfametafrontier(   formula    = log(PROD) ~ log(AREA) + log(LABOR) + log(NPK),   data       = ricephil,   group      = "group",   S          = 1,   udist      = "hnormal",   groupType  = "sfacross",   metaMethod = "qp" ) summary(meta_sfacross_qp)  ## 1c. sfacross groups + Two-stage SFA metafrontier (Huang et al., 2014) ##     The group-specific fitted frontier values serve as the dependent ##     variable in the second-stage SFA, yielding a stochastic technology gap. meta_sfacross_huang <- sfametafrontier(   formula     = log(PROD) ~ log(AREA) + log(LABOR) + log(NPK),   data        = ricephil,   group       = "group",   S           = 1,   udist       = "hnormal",   groupType   = "sfacross",   metaMethod  = "sfa",   sfaApproach = "huang" ) summary(meta_sfacross_huang) ef_huang <- efficiencies(meta_sfacross_huang)  ## 1d. sfacross groups + O'Donnell et al. (2008) stochastic metafrontier ##     The LP deterministic envelope is used as the second-stage dependent ##     variable: the metafrontier is estimated stochastically around the ##     envelope. meta_sfacross_odonnell <- sfametafrontier(   formula     = log(PROD) ~ log(AREA) + log(LABOR) + log(NPK),   data        = ricephil,   group       = "group",   S           = 1,   udist       = "hnormal",   groupType   = "sfacross",   metaMethod  = "sfa",   sfaApproach = "ordonnell" ) summary(meta_sfacross_odonnell)  ########################################################################### ## -------- SECTION 2: Latent Class (LCM) Group Frontier ---------------## ## No observed group variable: a pooled sfalcmcross model assigns       ## ## observations to 2 latent technology classes; these classes become the ## ## technology groups for the metafrontier.                               ## ###########################################################################  data("utility", package = "sfaR")  ## 2a. sfalcmcross (pooled, 2 classes) + LP metafrontier meta_lcm_lp <- sfametafrontier(   formula    = log(tc / wf) ~ log(y) + log(wl / wf) + log(wk / wf),   data       = utility,   S          = -1,   groupType  = "sfalcmcross",   lcmClasses = 2,   metaMethod = "lp" ) summary(meta_lcm_lp) ef_lcm_lp <- efficiencies(meta_lcm_lp) # Per-class posterior probabilities and class-specific efficiencies are # included alongside group and metafrontier efficiencies.  ## 2b. sfalcmcross (pooled, 2 classes) + QP metafrontier meta_lcm_qp <- sfametafrontier(   formula    = log(tc / wf) ~ log(y) + log(wl / wf) + log(wk / wf),   data       = utility,   S          = -1,   groupType  = "sfalcmcross",   lcmClasses = 2,   metaMethod = "qp" ) summary(meta_lcm_qp)  ## 2c. sfalcmcross (pooled, 2 classes) + Two-stage SFA metafrontier ##     (Huang et al., 2014) meta_lcm_huang <- sfametafrontier(   formula     = log(tc / wf) ~ log(y) + log(wl / wf) + log(wk / wf),   data        = utility,   S           = -1,   groupType   = "sfalcmcross",   lcmClasses  = 2,   metaMethod  = "sfa",   sfaApproach = "huang" ) summary(meta_lcm_huang) ef_lcm_huang <- efficiencies(meta_lcm_huang)  ## 2d. sfalcmcross (pooled, 2 classes) + O'Donnell et al. (2008) meta_lcm_odonnell <- sfametafrontier(   formula     = log(tc / wf) ~ log(y) + log(wl / wf) + log(wk / wf),   data        = utility,   S           = -1,   groupType   = "sfalcmcross",   lcmClasses  = 2,   metaMethod  = "sfa",   sfaApproach = "ordonnell" ) summary(meta_lcm_odonnell)  ########################################################################### ## -------- SECTION 3: Sample Selection SFA Group Frontier -------------## ## Simulated dataset with a Heckman selection mechanism. Only selected  ## ## observations (d == 1) participate in the frontier and metafrontier.  ## ## Efficiency estimates for non-selected observations are NA.           ## ###########################################################################  N <- 2000 set.seed(12345) z1 <- rnorm(N) z2 <- rnorm(N) v1 <- rnorm(N) v2 <- rnorm(N) g <- rnorm(N) e1 <- v1 e2 <- 0.7071 * (v1 + v2) ds <- z1 + z2 + e1 d <- ifelse(ds > 0, 1, 0) # binary selection indicator group <- ifelse(g > 0, 1, 0) # two technology groups (0 and 1) u <- abs(rnorm(N)) x1 <- rnorm(N) x2 <- rnorm(N) y <- x1 + x2 + e2 - u dat <- as.data.frame(cbind(y = y, x1 = x1, x2 = x2, z1 = z1, z2 = z2, d = d, group = group))  ## 3a. sfaselectioncross + LP metafrontier ##     Selection bias is corrected via the Greene (2010) two-step probit ##     approach. The LP envelope envelopes both groups' selected-sample ##     frontier fitted values. meta_sel_lp <- sfametafrontier(   formula    = y ~ x1 + x2,   selectionF = d ~ z1 + z2,   data       = dat,   group      = "group",   S          = 1L,   udist      = "hnormal",   groupType  = "sfaselectioncross",   modelType  = "greene10",   lType      = "kronrod",   Nsub       = 100,   uBound     = Inf,   method     = "bfgs",   itermax    = 2000,   metaMethod = "lp" ) summary(meta_sel_lp) ef_sel_lp <- efficiencies(meta_sel_lp)  ## 3b. sfaselectioncross + QP metafrontier meta_sel_qp <- sfametafrontier(   formula    = y ~ x1 + x2,   selectionF = d ~ z1 + z2,   data       = dat,   group      = "group",   S          = 1L,   udist      = "hnormal",   groupType  = "sfaselectioncross",   modelType  = "greene10",   lType      = "kronrod",   Nsub       = 100,   uBound     = Inf,   method     = "bfgs",   itermax    = 2000,   metaMethod = "qp" ) summary(meta_sel_qp)  ## 3c. sfaselectioncross + Two-stage SFA metafrontier (Huang et al., 2014) meta_sel_huang <- sfametafrontier(   formula     = y ~ x1 + x2,   selectionF  = d ~ z1 + z2,   data        = dat,   group       = "group",   S           = 1L,   udist       = "hnormal",   groupType   = "sfaselectioncross",   modelType   = "greene10",   lType       = "kronrod",   Nsub        = 100,   uBound      = Inf,   simType     = "halton",   Nsim        = 300,   prime       = 2L,   burn        = 10,   antithetics = FALSE,   seed        = 12345,   method      = "bfgs",   itermax     = 2000,   metaMethod  = "sfa",   sfaApproach = "huang" ) summary(meta_sel_huang) ef_sel_huang <- efficiencies(meta_sel_huang)  ## 3d. sfaselectioncross + O'Donnell et al. (2008) stochastic metafrontier meta_sel_odonnell <- sfametafrontier(   formula     = y ~ x1 + x2,   selectionF  = d ~ z1 + z2,   data        = dat,   group       = "group",   S           = 1L,   udist       = "hnormal",   groupType   = "sfaselectioncross",   modelType   = "greene10",   lType       = "kronrod",   Nsub        = 100,   uBound      = Inf,   method      = "bfgs",   itermax     = 2000,   metaMethod  = "sfa",   sfaApproach = "ordonnell" ) summary(meta_sel_odonnell) } # }

title: metafrontieR 1.0.0
text: First public release metafrontieR. Implements stochastic deterministic metafrontier analysis productivity performance benchmarking across firms operating different technologies. Standard SFA (sfacross) Latent class SFA (sfalcmcross) Sample-selection-corrected SFA (sfaselectioncross) Linear programming (LP) deterministic envelope Quadratic programming (QP) deterministic envelope Second-stage stochastic frontier (Huang et al. 2014; O’Donnell et al. 2008) Full efficiency outputs via efficiencies(): group TE (JLMS BC), metafrontier TE, metatechnology ratios (MTR). Five vignettes illustrating major use case.
